b'
Welcome back to Wall Power, your newly expanded-to-four-days-a-week private email about the art world and the deals and people who make it so fascinating and (sometimes) so very valuable. From here on, you\xe2\x80\x99ll be getting three emails a week from me\xe2\x80\x94often featuring my brand-new colleague Julie Brener Davich\xe2\x80\x94on Sunday, Tuesday, and Friday.

Jan 05, 2025

Marion Maneker

Happy New Year, sports fans. (Are we still saying that?) Anyway, welcome back to Wall Power, your newly expanded-to-four-days-a-week private email about the art world and the deals and people who make it so fascinating and (sometimes) so very valuable.

From here on, you\xe2\x80\x99ll be getting three emails a week from me\xe2\x80\x94often featuring my brand-new colleague Julie Brener Davich \xe2\x80\x94on Sunday, Tuesday, and Friday. Plus, Inner Circle members will get an exclusive Wednesday email. This upper tier of subscription also includes bimonthly calls with Puck authors, access to Puck merch, and even more detailed-to-wonky art data and analysis. Upgrade here .

We\xe2\x80\x99re working out the editorial rhythm in the next few weeks, so please don\xe2\x80\x99t hesitate to let us know what you like and want to read more of. You can reach me on Signal, WhatsApp, and text at 917.825.1391. (Try it out, I\xe2\x80\x99ll text you back.)

Now, let\xe2\x80\x99s get started\xe2\x80\xa6

  • Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s Madison blues : December was a rough month capping a rough year for Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s. The company announced last autumn that it had closed on the former site of the Whitney Museum\xe2\x80\x94the Breuer building on Madison Avenue\xe2\x80\x94whose brutalist exterior falls under landmarking laws that aim to preserve historic buildings. Now, it appears that NYC\xe2\x80\x99s Landmarks Preservation Commission has taken an interest in the interior as well. Last month, the commission announced it would schedule public hearings in the new year, which, according to The Patch , are a step toward granting landmark status to the inside of the building. If that\xe2\x80\x99s granted, Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s would have to get its interior redesign approved by the commission.
  • St. Barts\xe2\x80\x99 billionaire boat show : The holiday break always sends denizens of the art world scattering to a variety of destinations, whether humble or remote and exotic. On Instagram, I spotted some British art dealers and their families in France. Another American dealer, whose wife has her own gallery with partners, started in Paris before decamping to Morocco. Meanwhile, half the world seemed to be in Japan this year, due to the cheap yen against the dollar, including some collectors I know.

    But the New Year\xe2\x80\x99s place to be for the absurdly rich was, as is often the case, St. Barts. Looking at the AIS data\xe2\x80\x94yachting\xe2\x80\x99s version of tracking the tail numbers on private jets\xe2\x80\x94I could see a number of boat owners there who range from dabbling buyers to serious art collectors. There were nearly 200 yachts anchored off the 8-square mile island on December 31 to see the famous fireworks display. The usual suspects dropped anchor, including David Geffen , who may be one of the most successful collectors of the last 50 years. (If Geffen himself wasn\xe2\x80\x99t there, his boat Rising Sun certainly was.) Bernard Arnault \xe2\x80\x99s Symphony was anchored off Gustavia, too, not far from Koru, owned by Jeff Bezos , one of the few billionaires in Arnault\xe2\x80\x99s wealth class, whose name everyone mentions after Ken Griffin \xe2\x80\x99s when there\xe2\x80\x99s a really big-ticket artwork on offer.

    Other collecting yachties worth noting: Eyal Ofer , of the Ofer family of major collectors\xe2\x80\x94whose patriarch, Sammy Ofer , briefly partnered with the Mugrabi family 30 years ago\xe2\x80\x94had his blunt-bowed Olivia O there. David Reuben , one of the British brothers, whose niece Lisa Reuben is an active collector, was there on his boat, Siren , as was Michael Platt , the founder of BlueCrest Capital, who used to have a unique way of collecting art by only commissioning work directly, on Arrow . A whole Las Vegas contingent including Steve Wynn \xe2\x80\x99s Andrea was there, too, along with Viva , which belongs to Frank Fertitta , another casino owner with a deep interest in art, and Lonian , which belongs to his brother, Lorenzo . Cowboys owner Jerry Jones , who has a lot of contemporary art in his Dallas stadium, had his yacht, Bravo Eugenia there. Steven Spielberg 's Seven Seas was also there.

    Of course, we shouldn\xe2\x80\x99t ignore all of the Waltons \xe2\x80\x94cousins Nancy and Ann \xe2\x80\x94on their respective yachts, nor Lachlan Murdoch , Jan Koum , Barry Sternlicht , Michael Jordan , Eddie Lampert , Arthur Blank , James Dyson , or Yuri Milner , who also had boats in St. Barts.

Speaking of where in the world is the art world\xe2\x80\xa6

The Art World\xe2\x80\x99s New Manhattan Clubhouse

The folks at Hauser & Wirth want you to experience the art collecting lifestyle in a down-to-earth, casual setting, complete with a very mindful seasonal locavore menu. Can a simple concept of art gallery-as-restaurant bring new collectors into the fold?

Marion Maneker

After a day spent trudging from gallery to gallery, and with a couple of hours to kill before dinner, Mrs. Wallpower and I stopped in SoHo a couple weeks ago for a holiday drink at Manuela, the Hauser & Wirth restaurant that opened at Prince and Wooster in late October. Our companion, a glamorous young art advisor, had spent the day packing for a multi-leg holiday jaunt to Paris and England to meet her vaguely aristocratic (and newish) boyfriend and his family. Together, the three of us were the exact target clientele for Manuela, where the vibe is meant to be \xe2\x80\x9cart-world hangout,\xe2\x80\x9d and where industry players (the glamorous art advisor) rub shoulders with the curious (me and my wife) and the aspirational (most of the folks at the bar).

Manuela is designed to serve as \xe2\x80\x9ca democratic space that doesn\xe2\x80\x99t feel like it has the pomp and ceremony that some places have,\xe2\x80\x9d Bee Emmott , the C.E.O. of Artfarm, Hauser\xe2\x80\x99s sister hospitality company, told Vogue . Presumably she was talking about other restaurants, but you could apply the same sentiment to the entire gallery world, where self-serious is the default mode, and where everyone claims to want new clients, but remains wary of investing time and manners into anyone who hasn\xe2\x80\x99t already demonstrated commitment to the game. Here, though, all it takes is a reservation to grant you proximity to the world of serious art collecting.

A similar idea drives Artfarm, Hauser\xe2\x80\x99s extensive program to make art less remote and foreboding by pairing it with hospitality. Strategically, combining art and hospitality\xe2\x80\x94from the gallery side\xe2\x80\x94has been one of the few new business innovations in art dealing since the emergence of the global gallery decades ago. Hauser\xe2\x80\x99s own expansion into food and lodging began with a Somerset, U.K., farm and gallery. Artfarm also now runs the Groucho Club, in London, as well as restaurants and hotels in multiple locations, combining the experience of food, art, and travel. These include the original Manuela, which opened in the Hauser & Wirth complex in Los Angeles in 2016; restaurants and pubs in London and Somerset; plus destinations in Scotland and Minorca. There are also plans for a Groucho Club near the Yorkshire sculpture park, where there\xe2\x80\x99s already a branch of Soho House. (The Wirths \xe2\x80\x94husband and wife partners Iwan and Manuela \xe2\x80\x94aren\xe2\x80\x99t the only ones hip to the appeal of art as a travel beacon.)

By making art more of an everyday experience, Artfarm is essentially doing the reverse of what Peter Marino did with a slew of fashion boutiques and the flagship Tiffany store. There, the architect used art to make clothes and handbags seem loftier and more refined\xe2\x80\x94 i.e. , not just very expensive consumer goods: They were now art-adjacent, artlike , or just plain art. Artfarm, by contrast, is making art less intimidating and inscrutable, safer for consumption.

The commercial logic here is straightforward, even if it\xe2\x80\x99s not the only reasoning: There\xe2\x80\x99s a new generation of potential art buyers who have to get used to these objects, and who, while they can afford the luxury travel and hospitality, find the idea of ownership too remote in a social sense, or too aspirational in a financial sense. And, maybe more importantly and less obviously, Hauser & Wirth is building a potentially valuable business off the untapped resource of the gallery\xe2\x80\x99s stable of artists, estates, and their inventory.

At Manuela, as at other Artfarm restaurants, museum/gallery quality art is hanging on the walls, up close and personal. At, say, the Met, you might stroll by Both , a Philip Guston donated by the artist\xe2\x80\x99s daughter, glance at the wall tag and, if you\xe2\x80\x99re lucky and diligent, linger a few minutes. At Manuela, though, you might be seated right under Crescent , a work by the same artist, of similar size and composition, completed just before Both in 1976\xe2\x80\x94or maybe under the Cindy Sherman centerfold Untitled #92 (two examples have sold for more than $2 million at auction); or a Nicolas Party landscape (a similar work made $3.2 million at auction three years ago). You can spend an hour or more looking at that Louise Bourgeois spider, the Mika Rottenberg vine-and-recycled-plastic chandelier and candelabra at the bar, or the Pat Steir painting in the private dining room, where you\xe2\x80\x99ll eat off a Rashid Johnson mosaic piece.

In short, the place lets the art creep up on you and hit you when you least expect it, which is the kind of experience that you can\xe2\x80\x99t get anywhere else\xe2\x80\x94not at a gallery or a museum\xe2\x80\x94unless you happen to own art yourself.\xc2\xa0And that\xe2\x80\x99s the point. At Manuela, the art is the experience.

The Clubhouse

I was reminded of all of this as I read New York \xe2\x80\x99s review of the restaurant, which dwells more on the decor than the food. \xe2\x80\x9cOh, right\xe2\x80\x94the food,\xe2\x80\x9d wrote the magazine\xe2\x80\x99s critic, Matthew Schneier , who found many of the dishes to be \xe2\x80\x9cless creative than their surroundings,\xe2\x80\x9d but declared it \xe2\x80\x9cperfectly good\xe2\x80\x9d even if it\xe2\x80\x99s largely \xe2\x80\x9cexactly what you would expect\xe2\x80\xa6 a mid-career survey of localish seasonal eateries.\xe2\x80\x9d

The New York Manuela serves a different function than its Los Angeles cousin, which acts more like a museum concession\xe2\x80\x94think Caf\xc3\xa9 Sabarsky at Neue Galerie, or Frenchette at the Whitney\xe2\x80\x94an amenity that eases the trip to an unfamiliar neighborhood or an intimidating institution. That open-air restaurant provides a palate cleanser for the senses and facilitates spending a few hours in Hauser\xe2\x80\x99s art complex.

But here in New York, where Hauser\xe2\x80\x99s gallery spaces are distributed throughout the city\xe2\x80\x94Chelsea, the Upper East Side, and a few steps north of Manuela on Wooster\xe2\x80\x94the restaurant would ideally become a clubhouse: a place where artists, collectors, advisors, and maybe even a few rival dealers will congregate and table hop. That would give the art experience authenticity and appeal.

In the meantime, Mrs. Wallpower, the art advisor, and I spent several hours entertained by a charming bartender and taking side-eye notes on the handsy couple seated next to us. If I\xe2\x80\x99m being honest, the bar seemed more lively than the restaurant. And the overall effect of so much art, and so many art-based design objects, crammed into one experience could come across as contrived. That said, when I messaged the art advisor to find out what she thought, she told me she was going back again this week to try the food.

Endnotes\xe2\x80\xa6

In the pre-holiday frenzy with the added drama at Sotheby's, I\xe2\x80\x99d only glanced at this odd New York Times story identifying\xe2\x80\x94sort of\xe2\x80\x94the family that now owns the storied 1890 van Gogh painting, Portrait of Dr. Gachet . This was one of the last works made by the artist, and its purchase marked the peak of the Japanese art-buying frenzy of the late 1980s and into early 1990, when it was auctioned at Christie\xe2\x80\x99s for $82.5 million ($204.3 million in today\xe2\x80\x99s dollars).

I\xe2\x80\x99m not sure the Times understands that outing the very discreet Italian-Swiss family that probably owns the painting\xe2\x80\x94they didn\xe2\x80\x99t deny it through their lawyers\xe2\x80\x94isn\xe2\x80\x99t really news. If the family decides to sell, that\xe2\x80\x99s a story. But owning a major van Gogh for nearly 30 years, and never letting anyone see it, creates the sort of mystique that makes some works of art so valuable.

The far more interesting part of the painting\xe2\x80\x99s provenance concerns the man who owned the work briefly in the late 1990s\xe2\x80\x94but never saw it. Wolfgang Fl\xc3\xb6ttl , the now-disgraced Austrian hedge fund manager, lived a dangerous financial life built upon secretly borrowed money and social climbing. ( The Wall Street Journal published a good overview in 2007, when Fl\xc3\xb6ttl\xe2\x80\x99s scheme unraveled.) Before then, in 1997, Fl\xc3\xb6ttl had been acquiring art partially funded by credit from Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s. When he bought the van Gogh, his debt to Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s ballooned to $240 million. He couldn\xe2\x80\x99t make payments in the wake of the Long-Term Capital collapse, however, and Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s started selling his paintings. That\xe2\x80\x99s how Portrait of Dr. Gachet wound up in Switzerland\xe2\x80\xa6 or wherever it is.

Okay, that\xe2\x80\x99s enough fast and loose international finance for one day. I\xe2\x80\x99m glad to be back in the harness and eager to have Julie come help pull the sled. As always, you can just hit reply to this email if you have questions you want answered or have something you want us to look into. Your tips and curiosity are central to what we do.

Let\xe2\x80\x99s check back in on Tuesday.

M

Puck founding partner Matt Belloni takes you inside the business of Hollywood, using exclusive reporting and insight to explain the backstories on everything from Marvel movies to the streaming wars.

Lauren Sherman, a former top executive at Business of Fashion, is the preeminent fashion journalist of her generation. Line Sheet offers incisive reportage on all aspects of the industry and its biggest players. Every Wednesday, Lauren is joined by the beauty industry\xe2\x80\x99s leading reporter, Rachel Strugatz, who writes the definitive guide to what\xe2\x80\x99s happening across the category, from M&A to executive shuffles.

Politico, Brother, Where Art Thou?

DYLAN BYERS

Biden & His Discontents

TARA PALMERI

Play It as It Lays

JOHN OURAND
